extra.reasoning:
The property requires globally (opted_out implies not sent): in every reachable state where opted_out is true, sent must be false. Transition t5 (on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 1): sent := true) directly contradicts this by setting sent=true while opted_out is true, provided ticks has reached 1.

Counterexample execution (3 events, within the 5-event bound) from initial state (opted_out=F, sent=F, ticks=0):
1. tick -> t4 fires: ticks=1, sent=false. State: (opted_out=F, sent=F, ticks=1).
2. optout -> t1 fires: opted_out=true, sent=false. State: (opted_out=T, sent=F, ticks=1).
3. send_attempt -> guard of t5 (opted_out and ticks>=1) holds, so t5 fires: sent:=true. State: (opted_out=T, sent=T, ticks=1).

The resulting state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, violating the safety property. Although t3 (send_attempt if opted_out: sent:=false) also matches on send_attempt, t5 is an enabled transition that explicitly sets sent:=true under exactly these guards, so the violating state is reachable. The other transitions (t1, t3, t4) all clear sent to false and are safe; t2 only sets sent=true when not opted_out. The defect is the special-case t5 that re-enables sending after a tick despite opt-out.
